course.title: HTTP Servers in TypeScript: Node 24 and Express 5 Production APIs
course.description: TypeScript developers who can build robust HTTP clients and relational data systems and now need production-grade Node and Express origin services. This original, research-backed path uses repeated retrieval, cumulative artifacts, changed-case labs, stakeholder projects, and a performance exam.
outcomes: Demonstrate node 24.18 lts, npm 12, typescript 7 native and typescript 6 compatibility toolchains, node http, express 5.2.1, zod 4.4, node-postgres 8.22, jose 6.2, http semantics, routing, middleware, validation, bodies, streaming, async context, cancellation, concurrency, postgresql, consistency, authentication, authorization, browser and proxy trust, webhooks, ssrf, smuggling defense, node:test, supertest, fuzzing, opentelemetry 1.43, profiling, openapi 3.2, deployment, draining, and recovery through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

Demonstrate runnable deterministic pure-typescript decision functions plus explicit node, express, listener, tls, postgresql, proxy, worker, load, and production transfer gates through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.
Demonstrate five cumulative production service projects and a performance-based certification exam through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.
sharedRequirements: Every activity retrieves strict TypeScript, HTTP client, SQL, accessibility, security, cancellation, testing, and causal-evidence habits before adding one server boundary.

Browser TypeScript is deterministic and isolated. It models request, middleware, policy, dependency, response, and cleanup decisions but never imports Node or Express, opens a listener, contacts a network or database, spawns a worker, executes host commands, reads host state, or handles real credentials.

Passing work requires exact request and route inputs, trusted authority, type and runtime validation, resource budgets, response and durable-effect evidence, changed and failure cases, cleanup and recovery, and explicit full-stack transfer limits.

modules.description: The origin and proxy disagree about framing, forwarded fields are appended without trust, hop-by-hop fields cross boundaries, and parser tolerance is enabled to fix malformed clients. Learners cumulatively produce proxy hop, framing, and parser-consistency contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Strip and regenerate hop-by-hop fields per connection, control Host and authority, handle Connection-nominated fields, and document request and response rewriting.
Require trusted proxies to overwrite client-controlled Forwarded and X-Forwarded values and validate the observed hop count against every reachable topology.
Reject conflicting Content-Length, transfer-coding ambiguity, invalid field syntax, premature EOF, excess body, and front-end or origin disagreement.

modules.title: node:test, Supertest, Contracts, Properties, Fuzzing, and Fault Injection
modules.description: Tests call live dependencies, assert only status 200, share mutable state, never vary malformed inputs, and mistake mocks or coverage for behavioral proof. Learners cumulatively produce layered deterministic server verification matrix through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Use node:test suites, subtests, assertions, fixtures, hooks, concurrency, timers, and cleanup to prove domain decisions independently of Express.
Exercise Express handlers with Supertest using exact methods, paths, fields, bodies, identity, effects, errors, and cleanup without unnecessary public listeners.
Verify runtime requests and responses against schemas and consumer cases while detecting undocumented statuses, fields, nullability, security, and behavioral drift.

modules.title: Async Context, Logs, Metrics, Traces, Health, and Incident Evidence
modules.description: The service logs secrets and raw paths, labels metrics by identifiers, loses trace context, reports healthy during saturation, and cannot connect an alert to a user outcome. Learners cumulatively produce bounded telemetry and health evidence contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Emit structured events with timestamp, severity, stable event name, bounded route, request and trace correlation, outcome, latency, and field-level redaction.
Define request, error, duration, saturation, queue, pool, event-loop, process, and business metrics with bounded labels and histogram semantics.
Create OpenTelemetry server spans with stable route names, span kind, method, status, error, network, server, propagation, sampling, and sensitive-data policy.

modules.title: Profiling, Event-Loop Health, Memory, Load, and Capacity
modules.description: A team optimizes microbenchmarks, measures only average latency, ignores coordinated omission and warmup, raises concurrency until throughput peaks, and has no resource or cost model. Learners cumulatively produce performance experiment and capacity envelope through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Translate user latency and throughput outcomes into request-path budgets, resource hypotheses, experiment controls, and stop conditions before optimizing.
Use CPU profiles, heap snapshots, allocation, garbage collection, event-loop delay, handles, and request traces to locate causal bottlenecks safely.
Model arrival rate, concurrency, payload, endpoint mix, warmup, caches, connection reuse, percentiles, coordinated omission, errors, and saturation.

modules.title: OpenAPI 3.2, Compatibility, Deployment, Draining, and Recovery
modules.description: Documentation differs from runtime, examples contain secrets, additive changes break strict clients, storage contracts before old revisions drain, and rollback cannot restore invariants. Learners cumulatively produce versioned API contract and production release defense through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Describe operations, parameters, request bodies, responses, schemas, security, callbacks, webhooks, examples, links, servers, and reusable components in OpenAPI 3.2.
Choose a source of truth and gate generated clients, runtime validation, examples, operation IDs, security behavior, unknown fields, and spec-to-service drift.
Classify additive and breaking changes across fields, nullability, validation, ordering, status, errors, authentication, caching, timing, and business behavior.
